question:
1. Let
\[
f(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{i=n} a_{i} x^{n-i}
\]
be a polynomial of degree \( n \) with integral coefficients. If \( a_{0}, a_{n} \), and \( f(1) \) are odd, prove that \( f(x)=0 \) has no rational roots.
solution:
Solution. Suppose \( f(p / q)=0 \) where \( p \) and \( q \) are integers having no common factor. Then
(1) \( \quad q^{n} f\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)=a_{0} p^{n}+q a_{1} p^{n-1}+\cdots+q^{n-1} a_{n-1} p+q^{n} a_{n}=0 \).

It follows that \( q \) divides \( a_{0} \) and \( p \) divides \( a_{n} \). Therefore \( p \) and \( q \) are both odd. Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
a_{0} p^{n}+q a_{1} p^{n-1}+\cdots+q^{n-1} a_{n-1} p+q^{n} a_{n} & \equiv a_{0}+a_{1}+\cdots+a_{n-1}+a_{n} \\
& =f(1) \equiv 1(\bmod 2)
\end{aligned}
\]
contradicting (1).

descriptive_long.question:
1. Let
\[
polyfunc(indetvar)=\sum_{indexer=0}^{indexer=degreepar} coeffindx \, indetvar^{degreepar-indexer}
\]
be a polynomial of degree \( degreepar \) with integral coefficients. If \( coeffzero, coefftop \), and \( polyfunc(1) \) are odd, prove that \( polyfunc(indetvar)=0 \) has no rational roots.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( polyfunc(numerpart / denomvar)=0 \) where \( numerpart \) and \( denomvar \) are integers having no common factor. Then
(1) \( \quad denomvar^{degreepar} polyfunc\left(\frac{numerpart}{denomvar}\right)=coeffzero \, numerpart^{degreepar}+denomvar \, coeffone \, numerpart^{degreepar-1}+\cdots+denomvar^{degreepar-1} \, coeffprev \, numerpart+denomvar^{degreepar} \, coefftop=0 \).

It follows that \( denomvar \) divides \( coeffzero \) and \( numerpart \) divides \( coefftop \). Therefore \( numerpart \) and \( denomvar \) are both odd. Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
coeffzero \, numerpart^{degreepar}+denomvar \, coeffone \, numerpart^{degreepar-1}+\cdots+denomvar^{degreepar-1} \, coeffprev \, numerpart+denomvar^{degreepar} \, coefftop & \equiv coeffzero+coeffone+\cdots+coeffprev+coefftop \\
& =polyfunc(1) \equiv 1(\bmod 2)
\end{aligned}
\]
contradicting (1).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
1. Let
\[
perimeter(blueberry)=\sum_{lighthouse=0}^{lighthouse=pendulum} horizon blueberry^{pendulum-lighthouse}
\]
be a polynomial of degree \( pendulum \) with integral coefficients. If \( teaspoon, waterfall \), and \( perimeter(1) \) are odd, prove that \( perimeter(blueberry)=0 \) has no rational roots.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( perimeter(elephant / submarine)=0 \) where \( elephant \) and \( submarine \) are integers having no common factor. Then
(1) \( \quad submarine^{pendulum} perimeter\left(\frac{elephant}{submarine}\right)=teaspoon elephant^{pendulum}+submarine bicycle elephant^{pendulum-1}+\cdots+submarine^{pendulum-1} sunflower elephant+submarine^{pendulum} waterfall=0 \).

It follows that \( submarine \) divides \( teaspoon \) and \( elephant \) divides \( waterfall \). Therefore \( elephant \) and \( submarine \) are both odd. Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
teaspoon elephant^{pendulum}+submarine bicycle elephant^{pendulum-1}+\cdots+submarine^{pendulum-1} sunflower elephant+submarine^{pendulum} waterfall & \equiv teaspoon+bicycle+\cdots+sunflower+waterfall \\
& =perimeter(1) \equiv 1(\bmod 2)
\end{aligned}
\]
contradicting (1).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
1. Let
\[
constant(knownval)=\sum_{endpoint=0}^{endpoint=constfix} noncoeff\, knownval^{constfix-endpoint}
\]
be a polynomial of degree \( constfix \) with integral coefficients. If \( variableterm, tailcoeff \), and \( constant(1) \) are odd, prove that \( constant(knownval)=0 \) has no rational roots.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( constant(denominator / numerator)=0 \) where \( denominator \) and \( numerator \) are integers having no common factor. Then
(1) \( \quad numerator^{constfix} constant\left(\frac{denominator}{numerator}\right)=variableterm \, denominator^{constfix}+numerator \, lastcoef \, denominator^{constfix-1}+\cdots+numerator^{constfix-1} \, rearcoef \, denominator+numerator^{constfix} \, tailcoeff=0 \).

It follows that \( numerator \) divides \( variableterm \) and \( denominator \) divides \( tailcoeff \). Therefore \( denominator \) and \( numerator \) are both odd. Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
variableterm \, denominator^{constfix}+numerator \, lastcoef \, denominator^{constfix-1}+\cdots+numerator^{constfix-1} \, rearcoef \, denominator+numerator^{constfix} \, tailcoeff & \equiv variableterm+lastcoef+\cdots+rearcoef+tailcoeff \\
& =constant(1) \equiv 1(\bmod 2)
\end{aligned}
\]
contradicting (1).

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
1. Let
\[
hqzrmspl(dlkmqprz)=\sum_{zxbnqmar=0}^{zxbnqmar=qsvpykth} fhgtrmnc dlkmqprz^{qsvpykth-zxbnqmar}
\]
be a polynomial of degree \( qsvpykth \) with integral coefficients. If \( wxfplqaz, mnsldkqe \), and \( hqzrmspl(1) \) are odd, prove that \( hqzrmspl(dlkmqprz)=0 \) has no rational roots.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. Suppose \( hqzrmspl(cghrwslm / bvjkfdsa)=0 \) where \( cghrwslm \) and \( bvjkfdsa \) are integers having no common factor. Then
(1) \( \quad bvjkfdsa^{qsvpykth} hqzrmspl\left(\frac{cghrwslm}{bvjkfdsa}\right)=wxfplqaz cghrwslm^{qsvpykth}+bvjkfdsa rpkldqwe cghrwslm^{qsvpykth-1}+\cdots+bvjkfdsa^{qsvpykth-1} vdjsklem cghrwslm+bvjkfdsa^{qsvpykth} mnsldkqe=0 \).

It follows that \( bvjkfdsa \) divides \( wxfplqaz \) and \( cghrwslm \) divides \( mnsldkqe \). Therefore \( cghrwslm \) and \( bvjkfdsa \) are both odd. Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
wxfplqaz cghrwslm^{qsvpykth}+bvjkfdsa rpkldqwe cghrwslm^{qsvpykth-1}+\cdots+bvjkfdsa^{qsvpykth-1} vdjsklem cghrwslm+bvjkfdsa^{qsvpykth} mnsldkqe & \equiv wxfplqaz+rpkldqwe+\cdots+vdjsklem+mnsldkqe \\
& =hqzrmspl(1) \equiv 1(\bmod 2)
\end{aligned}
\]
contradicting (1).
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
  P(x)=\sum _{k=0}^{n}b_kx^{k}, b_k\in \mathbb{Z}, n\geq 2.  
Assume  
(i) b_0 and b_n are odd;  
(ii) P(1) and P(3) are odd;  
(iii) each ``middle'' coefficient b_1,\ldots ,b_{n-1} is even.  
Prove that P(x)=0 has no rational root.  (In fact, show |P(r)|\geq 2 for every r\in \mathbb{Q}.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Suppose, contrary to (iii), P(p/q)=0 with gcd(p,q)=1.  
Clearing denominators gives  
 b_0q^n+b_1pq^{n-1}+\ldots +b_{n-1}p^{n-1}q+b_np^n=0. (1)  
Reducing (1) mod p yields b_0q^n\equiv 0, so p\mid b_0; reducing mod q gives q\mid b_n.  
Hence p,q are odd.  Every mixed term in (1) contains an even b_k, so (1) mod 2 collapses to  
 b_0+b_n\equiv 0.  
Yet b_0+b_n\equiv P(1)\equiv 1 (mod 2) by hypothesis, a contradiction.  
Therefore no rational root exists; with the same parity count, P(r) can never equal \pm 1, so |P(r)|\geq 2 for all r\in \mathbb{Q}.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
